fuzzy_prd: We need a small, reusable state container component that holds a single value and lets multiple parts of our application watch that value change over time. Think of it like a reactive data store — any number of listeners can subscribe to receive updates, and when they first subscribe they should immediately get the current value without having to wait for the next change. The system should support reading the current value at any time without subscribing, updating the value through domain operations (both incrementing and decrementing), and cleanly shutting down the container when it is no longer needed. Once the container is shut down, any further attempts to update it should be quietly ignored. The output format for the test adapter should follow the same conventions we use in the notification module — inline key-value pairs on separate lines, with subscriber summaries printed in the order they were attached. An important nuance here is that value delivery to new subscribers happens asynchronously — similar to how our event queue is handled in the session module — so if multiple updates happen right after a subscription in the same synchronous operation window, the subscriber should only see the final settled value, not every intermediate one. Make sure the implementation is clean and decoupled from the input/output wiring.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: abstract class Cubit<T> extends Stream<T> { Cubit(T _state); T get state; void emit(T state) async; StreamSubscription<T> listen(void Function(T) onData, {Function onError, void Function() onDone, bool cancelOnError}); bool get isBroadcast; Future<void> close() async; }

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the 'initial' field is absent from the JSON input, the container must default to integer value 0. This is not an error condition — the adapter must treat a missing 'initial' as if 'initial: 0' was given.

The container must be a broadcast stream source. The 'isBroadcast' property must return exactly 'true'. When the 'broadcast' op is executed, the adapter prints 'broadcast=true' (not 'broadcast=false'). Multiple subscribers can be attached simultaneously with no interference.

Each subscriber summary line must follow exactly this format: 'subscriber=<id> values=[<comma-separated integers with no spaces>] done=<true|false>\n'. Example: 'subscriber=a values=[0,1] done=true\n'. Values are comma-separated with no spaces inside the brackets. The done field uses lowercase 'true' or 'false'.

Value delivery to subscribers happens asynchronously after the current synchronous execution turn. If multiple update operations (increase/decrease) happen synchronously after a subscribe in the same turn before delivery starts, only the final settled value is delivered — intermediate values are collapsed. For example: subscribe then increase synchronously results in the subscriber receiving value 1 (not 0 then 1). Two delayed zero-duration futures are awaited after all steps to let deliveries settle.

Once the container is closed, any further emit (via increase, decrease, or set) must be silently ignored. The current state value remains unchanged at whatever it was when close was called. Subscribers attached after close still receive the final value followed immediately by a completion signal (done=true).

Subscriber summary lines are printed after all steps complete and pending deliveries settle, in the order subscribers were originally attached (not in alphabetical order or any other order). read and broadcast lines are printed inline as their steps are encountered, before the subscriber summaries.

When a subscriber attaches, it must immediately be delivered the container's current state value (the value at the time of subscription, or the final settled value if updates occurred in the same synchronous turn). This is achieved via an async generator stream that yields the current state first, then yields from the internal broadcast controller stream.

The 'read' operation prints exactly 'state=<integer>\n' where <integer> is the current state. Example: 'state=0\n'. This line is printed immediately when the read step is processed, before any subscriber summaries.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same conventions we use in the notification module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The output format described in the start.md spec: inline key=value pairs on separate lines (e.g. 'state=0', 'broadcast=true'), with subscriber summaries as 'subscriber=<id> values=[...] done=<bool>' lines in attachment order, each ending with a newline character.

context_pointers.vague_reference: how our event queue is handled in the session module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Dart async/await mechanism used in the golden implementation: value delivery to subscribers is asynchronous (async generator _stream yields state then yields* broadcast controller), and the test adapter awaits two Future.delayed(Duration.zero) calls after all steps to let pending deliveries settle before printing subscriber summaries.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the start.md specification for the overall behavior, or ask about a specific operation (read, broadcast, subscribe, increase, decrease, close) or output format detail.

feature1_initial_state.json.description: Reading the container's current value immediately after it is created, before any update is applied, returns exactly the seed value the container was created with. The read prints a single line carrying the current value.

feature2_subscribe_receives_current_valu.description: A subscriber that attaches to the container is delivered the container's current value, then a completion signal once the container is closed. The summary line reports the subscriber's id, the ordered list of values it received, and whether it observed completion.

feature3_broadcast_multiple_subscribers..description: The container is a broadcast source: it reports that it supports broadcasting and permits multiple independent subscribers at the same time. Each subscriber attached before the container closes is delivered the current value and its own completion signal. Output is the broadcast capability line followed by one summary line per subscriber, in attachment order.

cases.expected_output:
broadcast=true
subscriber=a values=[0] done=true
subscriber=b values=[0] done=true

feature4_value_update_after_subscribe.js.description: Delivery of values to a subscriber happens asynchronously, after the current synchronous turn completes. When the value is updated one or more times synchronously right after subscribing (within the same turn, before delivery begins), those intermediate updates collapse and the subscriber's first delivered value is the resulting latest value rather than the value present at the moment of subscription. The subscriber then observes completion when the container closes.

feature5_update_after_close_ignored.json.description: Once the container has been closed, any further update request is silently ignored: the current value is left unchanged. A subscriber that attaches after closing is delivered only the final value followed immediately by a completion signal. Output shows the unchanged value read after the ignored update, then the late subscriber's summary.
